subject.title: Mathematics Applications & Interpretation (AI)
subject.description:
**IB Mathematics: Applications and Interpretation** is a **Group 5** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **real-world applications of mathematics, statistical analysis, mathematical modeling, and data interpretation**, with a strong focus on **technology use, practical problem-solving, and interpreting mathematical results in context**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2021**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | External examination (calculator permitted) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 2** | External examination (technology required) | 1h 30min | 2h | SL: 40%, HL: 30% |
| **Paper 3** | External examination (technology required) | N/A | 1h | HL: 20% |
| **Mathematical Exploration** | Internal assessment | 30h | 30h | 20% |

strengths: Clear introduction, body and conclusion provide an adequate structure
Relevant material is presented in separate sections with headings and sub-headings
weaknesses: Figures and tables are mis-labelled or lack captions, disrupting coherence
Empty cells and garbled headers obscure data in tables
Logical flow is broken by digressions (card-game anecdote) and mis-ordered graphics
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration shows adequate organization and a recognizable progression (introduction â background â simulations â conclusion), but coherence is regularly disrupted by mis-placed figures, empty table entries and irrelevant anecdotes. The work is mostly relevant, yet contains repetition and unnecessary elements. These combined factors place Presentation solidly in band 2 (score 2).

strengths: Consistent use of standard notation (e.g. P(X=k), binomial formula) in key sections
Effective use of theoretical vs experimental tables and bar graphs to interpret results
Clear narrative comparing theoretical and observed probabilities enhances reader understanding
weaknesses: Occasional misprints in binomial coefficients and exponents reduce precision
Some graphs lack axes labels or legends
Figure and table references are sometimes out of order
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While there are notable mis-typings and inconsistencies in notation, the student employs appropriate mathematical language in most of the core analysis, and uses multiple representations effectively to compare theory and experiment. Communication is generally clear and consistent, warranting a band 3 assessment (score 3).

strengths: The personal context (phoning mother) drives the choice of simulations
Designing two distinct practical simulations (coin flips and dice rolls) shows initiative
The framing of the problem demonstrates significant independent thinking
weaknesses: The mathematical approach remains elementary and could be extended further
Connections to the studentâs broader interests or motivations are not deeply explored
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The exploration exhibits significant independent thinking and a clear personal approach through custom simulations, and demonstrates creativity and initiative in mapping real-life events to probabilistic models. These attributes align with band 2 (score 2).

strengths: Acknowledges limitations such as sample size and external factors
Brief comparison of theoretical and experimental results shows initial reflection
weaknesses: Reflections are superficial and not integrated into deeper critique
Little critical analysis of methodological assumptions
Connections to enhanced understanding remain minimal
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Reflection is present but limited in depth and critical analysis. Observations about model limitations are basic and not developed into meaningful critique of assumptions. This corresponds to band 1 (score 1).

strengths: Applies binomial distribution correctly in most cases and computes several probability tables accurately
Demonstrates good command of course-level mathematics and rigor in setting up simulations
Effectively maps real-world scenarios to appropriate mathematical models with only minor errors
weaknesses: A few calculations contain exponent or coefficient mis-applications
Sample sizes in dice trials are small, limiting statistical reliability
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student uses relevant SL-level mathematics rigorously, shows thorough understanding of the binomial model, and executes most calculations correctly, with only minor errors in some coefficients and exponents. The mapping of simulations to theory and the correct computation of experimental probabilities reflect sophistication above the middle band. These qualities align with a band 5 assessment (score 5).

strandSelections.reasoning: Designing two practical simulations (coin and dice) to test her hypothesis shows noticeable independent thinking beyond simply applying a textbook example, but the mathematics remains elementary and un-extended. This aligns with âsignificant evidenceâ (band 2) rather than âoutstanding.â

The student demonstrates a sound grasp of what a binomial model is and how to apply it to real and simulated data, but explanations remain routine and do not convey comprehensive insight or rigour. This matches âgood understanding in parts,â i.e., band 3.

Some calculations are correct, but there are notable errorsâincorrect binomial coefficient for 5 heads, misuse of exponents, and a mis-typed probability formulaâso accuracy is only partial. Band 2 reflects âmathematics is partially correct.â

Most of the material is related to estimating the probability that the mother answers, yet the write-up contains repetition (the definition of binomial distribution is given twice) and large blocks of irrelevant text/graphics. The work is therefore âmostly relevant with some unnecessary elements,â placing it firmly in band 2.

Standard notation such as \(P(X=k)\) and \(\binom{n}{k}\) is used, but inaccuracies (e.g. \({5\choose0.5}\), mismatched exponents) and occasional plain-text variables reduce precision. Thus the student shows âsome appropriateâ use rather than mostly or fully appropriate usage.

Explanations are readable, but notations, paragraphing and figure references are inconsistent; for instance, Table 2 is presented before its caption and some equations include typographical errors. Clarity is therefore mostly acceptable but not sustained, matching band 2.

The mathematics (basic binomial distribution, simple simulations) is fully within the SL syllabus and relevant to the question; no higher-level or sophisticated tools are introduced. This aligns with ârelevant mathematics at course level usedâ (band 3).

The exploration contains tables comparing theoretical and experimental probabilities and several bar charts; these representations are appropriate but simple, and some graphs lack axes labels or are duplicated. Hence only âsome appropriateâ multiple representations are evident.

The student briefly notes that time-zone differences and limited sample size may affect results, but the reflections are superficial and not integrated into deeper discussion. Limited reflection corresponds to band 1.

Using both a digital coin flip and a physical die to approximate a 25 % success probability shows initiative and some creativity, though the ideas remain fairly standard. Thus the strand fits âsignificant creativity and initiativeâ (band 2) but not the highest level.

The investigation follows a recognizable progression (introduction â background â two simulations â conclusion), so the reader can follow the argument. Nevertheless, the logical thread is frequently broken by unnecessary digressions (lengthy card-game anecdote, very long pasted figure strings) and several mis-ordered explanations (the coin table appears before the calculation it is supposed to illustrate). These weaknesses prevent a clear, seamless build-up of ideas, hence âsome coherence and logical developmentâ rather than the higher bands.

There is a basic comparison between theoretical and experimental probabilities, yet no detailed critique of assumptions or methodology. This is âbasic analysis with limited critique,â i.e., band 1.

Comments such as âthis reinforces the idea that probability models are simplifications of realityâ show a minimal connection to understanding, but the link is not explored in depth. Hence the descriptor for limited connection (band 1) applies.

Headings, sub-headings and separate tables/graphs show that basic structure is present; there is a discernible introduction, body and conclusion. However, tables are sometimes empty or mis-labelled, figures are inserted without captions, and the background mathematics is split awkwardly. Because the organization is only âadequateâ and not consistently clear, the strand fits band 2.

The choice of a deeply personal context (phoning her mother) clearly drives the exploration, and decisions such as mapping call outcomes to coin flips/dice rolls stem from that personal framing. This meets the descriptor for a âclear personal approach,â i.e., band 2.

annotations.comment: The cardâgame anecdote, while interesting, diverges from the main focus and disrupts coherence. Consider trimming or moving it to a reflection section.
The methodology section defines n and p but does not explain how p=0.25 was estimated from empirical data. Clarify the data source and calculation steps.
The example uses \({5\choose0.5}\) which is mathematically incorrect. It should read \(\binom{5}{5}\) for 5 successes out of 5 trials.
The block labeled âFigure 1â contains a stream of numbers without caption or explanation. Provide a descriptive caption and explain its relevance.
The introduction effectively grounds the exploration in a personal context, engaging the reader and demonstrating clear personal engagement.
The binomial probability formula $$P(X=k)=\binom{n}{k}p^k(1-p)^{n-k}$$ is stated correctly using proper notation.
Figure 7 lacks axes labels and units. Annotate each axis and include a legend to improve clarity.
The calculation of \(\binom{5}{4}=5\) is correctly shown, demonstrating understanding of binomial coefficients.
The text refers to \((4^5)\) outcomes for \(k=4\), which is incorrect. It should reference \(\binom{5}{4}\) not \(4^5\) for counting combinations.
The formula for \(P(X=4)\) has a misprint: it shows \(\frac{5}{4}\) instead of \(\binom{5}{4}=5\). Correct the coefficient and exponent terms.
The calculation for \(P(X=1)\) uses \(\binom{4}{1}\) and exponent 3, which is inconsistent with \(n=10\). Check and correct the parameters for the binomial formula.
The description of the coin flip simulator lacks details of the simulation parameters. Specify number of trials and how the simulator maps to the theoretical model.
Both inserted figures lack captions and are referenced out of order. Ensure each graphic has a clear caption and is introduced before use.
The trial table contains empty cells marked âIâ which obscure the results. Populate all entries correctly and use consistent symbols.
The narrative comparing theoretical and experimental values demonstrates clarity in interpreting results.
Table 2 is mentioned before a proper caption or label is provided. Place captions consistently above or below each table.
The comparison table and bar graph together offer effective multiple representations of theoretical vs experimental probabilities.
The bar graphs lack axis labels and units, making interpretation difficult. Add clear axes and legends.
After presenting the bar graphs, discuss in the text what patterns or deviations are evident compared to the theoretical model.
Figure 5 is included without clear introduction and caption placement. Ensure each figure is properly labeled before reference.
The interpretation of the discrepancy between expected and observed outcomes is clearly articulated, aiding reader understanding.
The mapping of dice outcomes to success (roll =6) is mentioned but not justified. Explain why this choice best represents p=0.25.
The dice trial table has only three repetitions, which produces a very small sample. Increase trial count to improve experimental reliability.
The assignment of sides 1â5 to failure and 6 to success may introduce bias. Discuss whether uniform randomness is preserved.
The experimental probability calculation \(P_{\rm exp}=6/30=0.20\) is correctly performed, showing accuracy in handling data.
The recomputation of \(P(X=1)\) uses exponent \(4-1\) instead of \(n-k=9\). Revisit the exponent to match \(n=10\).
Table of binomial probabilities for \(p=0.20\) repeats earlier values without discussing deviations. Add commentary on which k-values diverge substantially.
The suggestion to compare experimental probabilities \(p=1/6\) is useful; consider adding a direct side-by-side analysis in text.
The small table at the top displays garbled headers (e.g. â.n Ïâ, âÛâ). Replace with meaningful column headings.
The binomial probability table for \(p=1/6\) is correctly computed and shows command of course-level mathematics.
The conclusion integrates theory and experiment well and acknowledges model limitations, demonstrating good reflection.
Reflection mentions sample size and external factors but remains superficial. Develop a deeper critical analysis of assumptions and their impact.
Consider quantifying how increased sample size or inclusion of time-of-day data could reduce variance, strengthening the reflection.
